data.title: Spirit in Black, Chapter Five (Black Metal Edition)

data.text:
[wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink]'s unswerving commitment to blasphemy and extreme music, a catalog of well-regarded recordings, and a relentless touring schedule established them as one of Scandinavia's best known black metal acts. Though they began as a Swedish death metal outfit, by 1992's [wimpLink albumId="80534787"]Dark Endless[/wimpLink], their atmospheric debut album, they were already evolving sonically. 1996's iconic Heaven Shall Burn...When We Are Gathered firmly established them in black metal circles. 2002's [wimpLink albumId="52011358"]World Funeral[/wimpLink] offered a left turn toward accessibility, and 2004's [wimpLink albumId="178391736"]Plague Angel[/wimpLink] marked a return to form. The conceptual scope of 2007's [wimpLink albumId="178391667"]Rom 5:12[/wimpLink] was critically proclaimed a creative successor to [wimpLink artistId="3569642"]Celtic Frost[/wimpLink]'s classic [wimpLink albumId="75321362"]To Mega Therion[/wimpLink]. 2012's [wimpLink albumId="51970933"]Serpent Sermon[/wimpLink] showcased the band's maturity and a forceful new production style. 2015's acclaimed [wimpLink albumId="56246454"]Frontschwein[/wimpLink] and 2018's [wimpLink albumId="87784574"]Viktoria[/wimpLink] were each followed by multiple live outings, EPs, box sets, and singles. [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] released [wimpLink albumId="298072485"]Memento Mori[/wimpLink] in 2023.
The group formed under the leadership of guitarist Morgan Steinmeyer Håkansson in 1990 with vocalist [wimpLink artistId="11207481"]Andreas Axelsson[/wimpLink], bassist Richard Kalm, and drummer Joakim Grave rounding out the lineup. Their name is drawn from an ancient Mesopotamian deity and the patron saint of Babylon. They began as more of a standard death metal band with a black metal influence but have come to be known for their style of extreme black metal, which tends to focus on themes of warfare and anti-Christian sentiment and is characterized by relentlessly fast drumming and blurred walls of guitar noise.
They released their controversially titled demo tape Fuck Me Jesus in 1991. Soon after, a second guitarist was added, Devo Andersson, while Bogge Svensson replaced Kalm on bass. Using this lineup, [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] recorded their debut studio album, [wimpLink albumId="80534787"]Dark Endless[/wimpLink] for No Fashion Records. Unhappy with No Fashion, the band jumped to the French [wimpLink artistId="17452794"]Osmose Productions[/wimpLink], releasing [wimpLink albumId="21030809"]Those of the Unlight[/wimpLink] in 1993, this time with [wimpLink artistId="3531367"]Grave[/wimpLink] on drums as well as vocals and new bassist in B. War. For 1994's [wimpLink albumId="21173213"]Opus Nocturne[/wimpLink], Fredrik Andersson was brought in on drums, Devo Andersson left, and [wimpLink artistId="3531367"]Grave[/wimpLink] was resigned to vocals. (Meanwhile, [wimpLink artistId="17452794"]Osmose[/wimpLink] set about re-releasing the Fuck Me Jesus demo in 1995 -- at the time it was banned in seven countries.)
Lineup changes continued with [wimpLink artistId="3531367"]Grave[/wimpLink]'s subsequent departure; he was replaced by [wimpLink artistId="21446577"]Legion[/wimpLink] (ex-Opthalamia). This lineup issued their iconic, Heaven Shall Burn...When We Are Gathered in 1996. Universally acclaimed, the band quickly followed it with a live concert recording from the tour titled [wimpLink albumId="24985804"]Germania[/wimpLink]. Its claim to fame among fans and doubters was that it conclusively proved that [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] could sustain the nonstop, roaring blastbeat intensity on their records without studio trickery.
1998's Nightwing was partly inspired by the life of Vlad Tepes, aka Dracula. 1999's [wimpLink albumId="24986619"]Panzer Division [/wimpLink]was a war-themed concept album, their final release for [wimpLink artistId="17452794"]Osmose[/wimpLink]. After leaving the label, [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] set up their own label, Blooddawn (distributed in the U.S. by Century Media), to release the double-live Infernal Eternal and the full-length studio set [wimpLink albumId="52016914"]La Grande Danse Macabre[/wimpLink], in 2001.
[wimpLink artistId="12136517"]Emil Dragutinovic[/wimpLink] replaced Andersson on drums in 2002. The following year [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] released the more midtempo [wimpLink albumId="52011358"]World Funeral[/wimpLink]. It was perceived by some fans, as well as critics, as an attempt to break into the mainstream. Devo returned to the band in 2004 as bassist; he played on [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink]'s blasphemous [wimpLink albumId="178391736"]Plague Angel[/wimpLink], a theme the band would continue on their 2007 follow-up, [wimpLink albumId="178391667"]Rom 5:12[/wimpLink]. Following its release, critical and popular response to the latter album was unprecedented; many proclaimed it the aesthetic successor to [wimpLink artistId="3569642"]Celtic Frost[/wimpLink]'s 1985 black metal classic, [wimpLink albumId="75321362"]To Mega Therion[/wimpLink].
[wimpLink albumId="178391772"]Wormwood[/wimpLink], [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink]'s 11th album, appeared in 2009, followed shortly by [wimpLink albumId="51970933"]Serpent Sermon[/wimpLink] in 2012. Both albums showcased a new, more dynamically forceful -- though far from polished -- production style, amplifying the band's more extreme musical tendencies. The overt military themes on 2015's [wimpLink albumId="56246454"]Frontschwein[/wimpLink] harkened back to 1999's [wimpLink albumId="24986619"]Panzer Division[/wimpLink]. The following year, the band was forced to postpone their U.S. tour after being denied entry visas. Their militaristic lyrics and anti-Christian themes were cited as reasons. To fill the gap, [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] -- on their Blooddawn Productions label -- issued two live albums (Strigzscara Warwolf and World Panzer Battle 1999), two studio EPs (Ancient Evil, The Sun Turns Black at Night) and two singles ("Werwolf" and "Equestrian Bloodlust"). When they were finally allowed to continue their tour in North America, a 2017 Oakland, California show was cancelled after the venue was targeted by Antifa protestors who opposed the band's alleged sympathies for fascism.
Undeterred, they soldiered on and returned to the studio. In May 2018, they released the 7" single "Panzer Division Live" b/w "Baptism by Fire," followed by the long-player [wimpLink albumId="87784574"]Viktoria[/wimpLink] in June. The studio outing was a raw, back-to-basics black metal album that was more old-school than ever. 2019 saw the limited issue of The Official Bootleg Series, Vol. 1, a four-cassette boxed set containing two early albums and two EPS. They released Winged Death 1993, 7" EP containing live versions of "Wolves" and "On Darkened Wings." Bassist Devo left the band again in 2020 and was replaced by Joel Lindholm. Stalled by the pandemic's rampage the world over, [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] issued the live World Funeral: Jaws of Hell MMIII as a limited edition in October. In 2021, they released another archival concert date in Totentanz 2001. [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink] toured but were anxious to get back into the recording studio.
In May 2023, during the band's performance at Incineration Fest, bassist Lindolm was captured on a fan's video giving a Nazi salute from the stage. The band fired him immediately and brought back Devo. In August they released [wimpLink albumId="298072485"]Memento Mori[/wimpLink]. Produced and recorded by the band at Endarker Studios between September 2022 and March 2023, it marked [wimpLink artistId="24059"]Marduk[/wimpLink]'s first studio offering in five years. ~ William York & Thom Jurek
